mnemonics.title: CORES Speed Multiplier
mnemonics.content: CORES = Cache efficiency, Optimization, Replication, Execution, Synchronization - each factor contributes to multicore performance gains

mnemonics.title: GPU Parallel Power Phrase
mnemonics.content: Thousands Of Tiny Workers (Stream Processors) Operating In Unison (SIMD) Handle Graphics (G)Tasks Rapidly

mnemonics.title: Amdahl's Law Reminder
mnemonics.content: Serial Part Stays (S) = The serial portion of a program limits maximum speedup no matter how many cores added

mnemonics.title: Cache Memory Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: L1: Lightning Fast (Small, Close to CPU), L2: Moderate Speed (Medium), L3: Slower but Large (Shared among cores)

mnemonics.title: SIMD vs MIMD
mnemonics.content: SIMD = Single Instruction Marches Every Data (one command, many data), MIMD = Multiple Instructions Manage Individual Data (each core does different task)

acronyms.usage: Primary processor that executes instructions; in multicore systems, multiple CPUs work together
Specialized processor designed for parallel processing of graphics and computational tasks; contains thousands of small cores
Parallel computing paradigm where one instruction operates on multiple data points simultaneously; used in GPUs for graphics processing
Architecture where multiple processors execute different instructions on different data; used in multicore CPUs
Memory architecture where memory access time depends on memory location relative to processor; used in multi-socket systems
Memory architecture where all processors share equal access time to memory; used in symmetric multi-processing
Technology that allows multiple threads to execute simultaneously on a single core; increases resource utilization
Measure of processor efficiency; higher IPC means better performance per clock cycle
Parallelism achieved by executing multiple threads simultaneously across multiple cores
Parallelism within a single thread by executing multiple instructions in the same clock cycle
Parallel computing platform and API by NVIDIA for using GPUs for general-purpose computing
Framework for writing programs that execute across heterogeneous platforms (CPUs, GPUs, FPGAs)

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a multi-story office building where each floor (core) has its own team working on different tasks, but they share the same lobby (L3 cache) and entrance (system bus)
Picture a massive stadium crowd doing the wave (same instruction - SIMD) - thousands of people (cores) performing the same action at different sections (different data)
Think of a desk (L1 cache) with frequently used items, a nearby filing cabinet (L2) for less common items, and a warehouse (L3/main memory) for storage - each closer level is faster but smaller
Visualize a chain (program) with one weak link (serial part) - no matter how strong other links become (parallel parts), the chain breaks at the weakest point
Imagine team members passing notes through a central coordinator (bus) vs direct conversation (mesh network) - mesh is faster for many cores
Shared memory = one shared whiteboard everyone can see; Distributed memory = each person has their own notebook and must message others to share information

analogies.analogy: A restaurant kitchen with multiple chefs (cores) - each chef prepares different dishes (threads) simultaneously, increasing overall throughput; they share the same ingredients (shared memory) but need coordination to avoid conflicts

CPU is like a professor (few, complex, sequential tasks) - very smart but handles one complex problem at a time. GPU is like a thousand elementary school students (many simple cores) - each does simple arithmetic but together solve massive parallel problems quickly
Multiple people editing a shared Google Doc - cache coherency ensures everyone sees the latest version and prevents conflicts when multiple cores try to modify the same data

Four people painting different parts of the same wall - they need to coordinate (locks/semaphores) so they don't paint over each other's sections; without coordination, the result is messy (data race)
A highway with thousands of small cars (GPU data parallel) vs a few large trucks (CPU sequential) - GPU needs extremely wide memory bandwidth to handle massive simultaneous data access
Instead of one super-fast marathon runner (high-power single core), use many moderately fast walkers (lower-power cores) - together they cover more distance while using less total energy

An assembly line in a factory - vertices go through stages (vertex shader, rasterization, fragment shader) where each station performs a specific task before passing to the next, enabling parallel processing of millions of pixels

Trying to speed up a team project where one person must write the final report (serial part) - even if 9 other people do all research in parallel, the writer becomes the bottleneck limiting total speedup
